heroOverview: Porphyria describes a group of rare metabolic disorders in which deficiencies of specific enzymes in the heme biosynthesis pathway cause toxic accumulation of porphyrins or porphyrin precursors in the body. When the biochemical subtype has not yet been determined — or when the available workup cannot distinguish among subtypes — the ICD-10 code E80.20 (unspecified porphyria) is assigned. The porphyrias collectively affect an estimated 1 in 25,000 people in Western populations, though acute forms are probably under-diagnosed. Attacks can be life-threatening, triggered by common drugs, hormonal fluctuations, and fasting, and can mimic dozens of other conditions, making early specialist recognition critical.

keyStats.prevalence: Collectively, porphyrias affect approximately 1 in 25,000 people in Western populations; acute hepatic forms are more prevalent in northern Europe
keyStats.demographics: Acute porphyrias predominantly affect women of reproductive age (15–45 years); cutaneous forms affect both sexes equally across a wider age range
keyStats.avgAge: First acute attack typically occurs in the second or third decade of life; cutaneous forms may present at any age including childhood
keyStats.globalCases: Porphyria cutanea tarda is the most common form worldwide; acute intermittent porphyria has the highest prevalence in northern Europe, estimated at 1–2 per 100,000

definition: Porphyria, unspecified (ICD-10: E80.20) is assigned when the clinical and biochemical picture is consistent with a disorder of porphyrin metabolism but the specific enzymatic defect and subtype have not been established. The porphyrias arise from inherited or acquired partial deficiencies of one of eight enzymes in the heme biosynthesis pathway; the specific enzyme affected determines whether the disorder manifests primarily as acute neurovisceral attacks, cutaneous photosensitivity, or both. Heme — the iron-containing compound at the core of hemoglobin, myoglobin, and cytochrome P450 enzymes — is synthesised in a stepwise pathway beginning in the mitochondria and proceeding through the cytoplasm. When an enzyme step is impaired, substrate porphyrins or the precursors aminolevulinic acid (ALA) and porphobilinogen (PBG) accumulate to toxic levels in the liver, red blood cells, or both. The hepatic porphyrias (acute intermittent porphyria, hereditary coproporphyria, variegate porphyria, porphyria cutanea tarda) originate primarily in the liver; the erythropoietic porphyrias (congenital erythropoietic porphyria, erythropoietic protoporphyria) originate in bone marrow red cell precursors. Endocrinology and haematology manage porphyria; genetic medicine is involved when family screening is needed.

typesClassification.description: Include acute intermittent porphyria (AIP), hereditary coproporphyria (HCP), and variegate porphyria (VP); produce severe abdominal pain, neuropsychiatric symptoms, and autonomic dysfunction during attacks. AIP is the most common acute form in most populations.

The most common porphyria worldwide; caused by reduced hepatic uroporphyrinogen decarboxylase activity, producing photosensitivity, blistering, and skin fragility on sun-exposed areas without acute neurovisceral attacks.
Variegate porphyria and hereditary coproporphyria can cause both acute attacks and photocutaneous features, making them important differentials when both symptom types co-exist.

Congenital erythropoietic porphyria and erythropoietic protoporphyria arise from enzyme deficiency in bone marrow erythroid precursors; they present with marked photosensitivity from infancy or early childhood and can cause liver disease in the protoporphyric form.

primarySymptoms: Severe, diffuse abdominal pain — often the dominant symptom of an acute attack — that may last hours to days, with no peritoneal signs on examination, causing diagnostic confusion with surgical emergencies.
Neuropsychiatric manifestations during acute attacks including acute confusion, anxiety, hallucinations, and mood disturbance that can be mistaken for a primary psychiatric disorder.
Peripheral motor neuropathy — weakness, particularly of proximal muscles and the respiratory muscles — that can progress to respiratory failure in severe attacks.
Autonomic dysfunction including tachycardia, hypertension, sweating, urinary retention, and constipation during acute episodes.
Hyponatraemia from inappropriate ADH secretion (SIADH) — a potentially life-threatening electrolyte disturbance during acute attacks.
Dark, red-brown, or port-wine coloured urine due to excess porphyrin precursors, often most apparent after the urine stands and darkens on exposure to light.
Photosensitive skin lesions on sun-exposed areas — blistering, fragility, scarring, and hyperpigmentation — characteristic of cutaneous porphyrias or the cutaneous component of dual forms.
Severe pain and swelling of skin on sun exposure without blistering — a burning sensation characteristic of erythropoietic protoporphyria.
Weakness, fatigue, and haemolytic anaemia in erythropoietic porphyrias, where porphyrin accumulation damages red blood cells.
Hepatic involvement including abnormal liver enzymes and, in PCT and erythropoietic protoporphyria, progression to cirrhosis in a subset of patients.
earlyWarningSigns: New-onset episodic abdominal pain with no clear surgical or gastrointestinal cause, especially in young women.
Port-wine or dark urine appearing during or after a period of reduced food intake, illness, or new medication.
Increasing skin sensitivity to sunlight — blistering or scarring on hands or face — in the absence of another dermatological diagnosis.
Unexplained tachycardia or hypertension accompanying abdominal pain.
emergencySigns: Progressive limb weakness or difficulty breathing during an acute attack signals motor neuropathy with potential respiratory compromise — requires hospital admission and respiratory monitoring.
Severe hyponatraemia (sodium below 125 mmol/L) during an acute attack is a neurological emergency requiring controlled correction.
Seizures during an acute attack — anticonvulsants such as phenytoin and carbamazepine are porphyrinogenic and should be avoided; benzodiazepines and levetiracetam are safer alternatives.
Rapid deterioration in consciousness level in the context of abdominal pain, tachycardia, and dark urine requires immediate assessment for acute porphyria.

causes.cause: Inherited enzyme deficiency in the heme biosynthesis pathway
causes.description: Most acute porphyrias follow autosomal dominant inheritance with reduced penetrance; a heterozygous mutation in one of the pathway enzymes (e.g., HMBS for AIP, CPOX for HCP, PPOX for VP) reduces enzyme activity by approximately 50%, which is insufficient on its own to cause an attack but produces vulnerability to precipitating factors.

Many drugs induce hepatic cytochrome P450 enzymes, which upregulates ALA synthase 1 — the rate-limiting step in hepatic heme synthesis — overwhelming the residual enzyme capacity and flooding the pathway with toxic precursors; known triggers include barbiturates, sulfonamides, rifampicin, many anticonvulsants, and numerous others listed in dedicated drug databases.

Progesterone and its metabolites (5-beta reduced neurosteroids) upregulate hepatic ALA synthase 1; this explains why acute attacks are far more common in women, often occurring premenstrually or during hormonal contraceptive use, and why attacks rarely occur before puberty or after menopause.

Fasting reduces glucose availability, which normally suppresses ALA synthase 1 activity; reduced caloric intake of any cause — crash dieting, anorexia, perioperative fasting, or intercurrent illness — is one of the most common identifiable triggers of an acute attack.

In porphyria cutanea tarda, hepatic iron accumulation and alcohol damage reduce uroporphyrinogen decarboxylase activity below the threshold needed to prevent porphyrin accumulation; PCT is strongly associated with hepatitis C infection, excessive alcohol use, oestrogen-containing medications, and the HFE haemochromatosis genotype.

Intercurrent infections, surgery, and physical illness activate the stress-response axis, raise ALA synthase 1 activity via cortisol, and can precipitate acute attacks; this accounts for the common pattern of attacks occurring during or after an unrelated illness.

riskFactors.description: First-degree relatives of individuals with an acute hepatic porphyria have a 50% chance of inheriting the causative mutation; most gene carriers never have an attack but require drug-safety counselling.

Progesterone metabolites upregulate the porphyrogenic enzyme ALA synthase 1; symptomatic acute porphyria is approximately 5 times more common in women than men, with attacks clustering in the luteal phase.

Hundreds of drugs induce cytochrome P450 enzymes and upregulate heme synthesis; individuals with known or suspected porphyria must check every new medication against a specialist drug database such as the European Porphyria Network (EPNET) tool.

Both are major risk factors for porphyria cutanea tarda by reducing uroporphyrinogen decarboxylase activity in the liver; abstinence from alcohol and hepatitis C treatment are the primary treatments for PCT.
HFE mutations causing iron overload substantially increase the risk of porphyria cutanea tarda; iron deposition in the liver inhibits uroporphyrinogen decarboxylase directly.

riskFactors.factor: Caloric restriction or low carbohydrate diet
riskFactors.description: Ketogenic diets, fasting, and very low calorie intake remove glucose-mediated suppression of ALA synthase 1; individuals with acute porphyria should eat regular carbohydrate-containing meals and avoid prolonged fasting.

Combined oral contraceptives and postmenopausal hormone therapy containing oestrogen can precipitate acute attacks and worsen PCT; progesterone-only or non-hormonal contraception is generally safer in confirmed acute porphyria.

affectedDemographics: Women aged 15–45 for acute hepatic porphyrias — the group with the highest risk of symptomatic attacks
Both sexes equally for porphyria cutanea tarda, though alcohol-associated PCT is more common in men
Children and infants in erythropoietic porphyrias, which present from early childhood
Individuals of northern European descent for acute intermittent porphyria, where prevalence is highest
People with hepatitis C infection or HFE haemochromatosis mutations, who are at increased risk of PCT
diagnosisOverview: Porphyria is frequently delayed in diagnosis by years because its symptoms — especially in acute forms — mimic common conditions including acute abdomen, psychiatric disorders, and Guillain-Barré syndrome. The first-line test during a suspected acute attack is a spot urine sample for porphobilinogen (PBG); a markedly elevated urine PBG (typically more than 5 times the upper reference limit) in the setting of abdominal pain and neuropsychiatric symptoms strongly confirms an acute hepatic porphyria and justifies immediate treatment without waiting for full biochemical workup. Random urine PBG should be tested during symptoms rather than between attacks, when levels often normalise. Confirmatory subtype identification requires 24-hour urine porphyrins, plasma porphyrins, and stool porphyrins, with patterns distinguishing AIP from HCP and VP. Porphyria cutanea tarda is diagnosed by finding predominantly uroporphyrins and heptacarboxyl porphyrins in urine, with raised plasma fluorescence peak at approximately 620 nm. Genetic testing identifies the causative mutation and enables family screening. Between attacks, urine PBG can be normal in AIP, making biochemical diagnosis unreliable outside of symptomatic periods. All individuals with a confirmed or suspected porphyria diagnosis should be referred to a specialist centre with porphyria expertise. The differential diagnosis is broad and includes acute abdomen, Guillain-Barré syndrome, lead poisoning, and cyclic vomiting syndrome.

diagnosticTests.purpose: First-line test during a suspected acute attack; a markedly raised PBG confirms acute hepatic porphyria and supports immediate treatment.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A random urine sample collected during symptoms; the test takes a few hours in most laboratories. Refrigerate the sample and protect from light. Results above 5 times normal are clinically significant.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies and quantifies porphyrin fractions (uro-, hepta-, copro-, proto-) to determine the biochemical subtype.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: All urine collected over 24 hours in a dark, refrigerated container; the sample is sent to a specialist laboratory. Results require expert interpretation alongside clinical data.

diagnosticTests.test: Plasma porphyrins and fluorescence emission scan
diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects circulating porphyrins with a characteristic emission peak that helps distinguish porphyria cutanea tarda from variegate porphyria.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard blood draw analysed in a specialist laboratory using fluorescence spectroscopy; a plasma peak at 626 nm suggests VP while 619 nm is typical of PCT.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Measures coproporphyrin III and protoporphyrin fractions to distinguish hereditary coproporphyria and variegate porphyria from AIP.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A small stool sample collected in a light-protected container; requires a specialist laboratory.

diagnosticTests.test: Genetic (DNA) testing for HMBS, CPOX, PPOX, UROD mutations
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies the causative gene mutation, confirms the subtype definitively, and enables family screening.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A blood or saliva sample sent to a genetics laboratory; turnaround is typically 4–8 weeks; genetic counselling should accompany results.

diagnosticTests.test: Serum and urine ferritin, iron studies, HFE genotyping
diagnosticTests.purpose: Assesses iron overload as a contributing factor in suspected porphyria cutanea tarda.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: A standard blood draw; HFE genotyping is an add-on genetic test from the same sample.

diagnosticTests.test: Liver function tests, hepatitis C and B serology
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies hepatic disease contributing to PCT; hepatitis C is present in approximately 50–70% of PCT cases in some populations.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood tests available in most laboratories within 24–48 hours.

treatmentOverview: Treatment differs substantially between acute and cutaneous porphyrias. An acute attack requires hospital admission: intravenous glucose loading (300–500 g daily) suppresses ALA synthase 1 activity and is sufficient for mild attacks. For moderate to severe attacks, intravenous human hemin (heme arginate in Europe, hematin in North America) is the definitive treatment, providing exogenous heme to feedback-suppress ALA synthase 1 and rapidly reduce ALA and PBG levels; it should be given within 24–48 hours of a severe attack to prevent neuropathy progression. Precipitating factors must be removed immediately — porphyrinogenic drugs stopped, adequate caloric intake restored. Pain management uses opioids (morphine is safe), and nausea is treated with ondansetron or chlorpromazine (both safe). Patients with recurrent attacks despite avoidance of triggers may be candidates for givosiran, an RNAi therapeutic approved for acute hepatic porphyria that substantially reduces hepatic ALA synthase 1 expression; it reduces attack frequency by approximately 74% in trial data. Liver transplantation cures acute hepatic porphyria in the most severe refractory cases. For PCT, the mainstay of treatment is phlebotomy to reduce hepatic iron and low-dose hydroxychloroquine, which mobilises porphyrins from the liver; hepatitis C treatment with direct-acting antivirals achieves disease remission in PCT associated with hepatitis C. Photoprotection is essential in all cutaneous porphyrias. Afamelanotide — an alpha-melanocyte-stimulating hormone analogue — is approved in Europe for erythropoietic protoporphyria and increases pain-free sun exposure.

medicalTreatments.name: Intravenous hemin (heme arginate / hematin)
medicalTreatments.description: The definitive treatment for moderate to severe acute porphyria attacks; provides exogenous heme to suppress hepatic ALA synthase 1, rapidly lowering toxic precursor levels and halting neuropathy progression. Administered as a 4-day course intravenously via a central line because of thrombophlebitis risk.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Reduces urine ALA and PBG by 50–80% within 24–48 hours and halts neurological progression in the majority of acute attacks when given early.

medicalTreatments.name: Intravenous glucose loading
medicalTreatments.description: High-carbohydrate infusion (10% dextrose at a rate providing 300–400 g glucose/day) suppresses ALA synthase 1 via insulin signalling; used for mild attacks or while hemin is being arranged.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Effective for mild to moderate attacks; reduces attack severity within 24–48 hours in a majority of mild presentations but is insufficient alone for severe attacks with motor involvement.

medicalTreatments.name: Givosiran (Givlaari)
medicalTreatments.description: An RNA interference (RNAi) therapeutic given as a monthly subcutaneous injection that permanently reduces hepatic ALAS1 mRNA, preventing the enzyme induction that drives attacks; approved for adults with acute hepatic porphyria.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: In the ENVISION phase 3 trial, givosiran reduced the composite attack rate by approximately 74% versus placebo over 6 months; daily urine ALA levels fell by 59%.

medicalTreatments.name: Phlebotomy (therapeutic venesection)
medicalTreatments.description: Used in PCT to deplete hepatic iron stores; 500 mL blood is removed every 1–2 weeks until ferritin approaches the lower reference range; typically requires 4–10 sessions over months.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Skin lesions remit in approximately 80–90% of PCT patients following adequate iron depletion; remission is sustained for years in most cases.

medicalTreatments.name: Hydroxychloroquine (low-dose)
medicalTreatments.description: An alternative to phlebotomy or used in combination for PCT; low doses (100–200 mg twice weekly) mobilise hepatic porphyrins; full doses cause liver toxicity in PCT and must be avoided.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Comparable efficacy to phlebotomy in controlled trials; remission achieved in approximately 80% of PCT patients over 12–18 months of treatment.

alternativeTreatments.name: Gonadotropin-releasing hormone (GnRH) agonists for cyclical attacks
alternativeTreatments.description: In women with attacks tightly linked to the menstrual cycle (catamenial porphyria), GnRH agonists suppress ovarian hormone fluctuations, effectively preventing attacks; used as a bridge to givosiran or liver transplantation in severe cyclical cases.

alternativeTreatments.name: High-carbohydrate diet and avoidance of fasting
alternativeTreatments.description: Eating regular carbohydrate-rich meals and avoiding prolonged fasting suppresses ALA synthase 1 activity and is one of the most effective practical measures for reducing attack frequency in acute hepatic porphyria.

alternativeTreatments.name: Afamelanotide (Scenesse) for erythropoietic protoporphyria
alternativeTreatments.description: An alpha-melanocyte-stimulating hormone analogue implanted subcutaneously that increases photoprotective melanin production; approved in Europe for EPP, increasing pain-free sun exposure by approximately 26 minutes per day in trial data.

whySeeSpecialist: Porphyria is diagnosed and managed by specialists in porphyria, endocrinology, or haematology at centres with access to the appropriate biochemical assays and expert interpretation. General practitioners are unlikely to have the experience needed to interpret porphyrin fractions, prescribe hemin, initiate givosiran, or provide safe drug prescribing guidance. In an acute attack, immediate contact with a specialist centre can prevent avoidable neuropathy from unsafe drug use; European and North American porphyria networks provide 24-hour advice lines for emergencies.
doctorSelectionGuide: Seek an endocrinologist, haematologist, or metabolic physician at a designated porphyria centre; the European Porphyria Network (EPNET) and the American Porphyria Foundation maintain centre directories. For acute disease, confirm the centre can provide hemin infusions and rapid PBG testing. Genetic counselling should be integrated into the service for family screening. Patients taking multiple medications need a physician who will cross-check all prescriptions against porphyria drug databases at every visit.
preventionStrategies: Obtain an up-to-date porphyria drug-safety card listing unsafe medications; check every new prescription, including over-the-counter drugs and herbal supplements, against a specialist database (e.g., EPNET drug list).
Maintain regular carbohydrate intake — avoid prolonged fasting, skipping meals, or very low carbohydrate diets.
Abstain from alcohol or limit intake strictly in cutaneous forms; alcohol is both a trigger and a cause of PCT.
Avoid oestrogen-containing hormonal contraceptives if acute porphyria is confirmed; discuss progesterone-only or non-hormonal alternatives with a specialist.
Carry a medical alert card or bracelet identifying the condition so emergency staff can avoid unsafe treatments.
Family members of confirmed cases should undergo biochemical and genetic screening to identify asymptomatic carriers who need drug-safety counselling.
lifestyleModifications: Eat three regular meals daily with adequate carbohydrate content; do not diet aggressively or fast.
Avoid known alcohol triggers; if PCT is present, complete alcohol abstinence is recommended.

Use physical photoprotection — long sleeves, wide-brim hats, SPF 50+ sunscreen — for cutaneous porphyrias, as standard glass and most sunscreens do not block the relevant Soret band wavelengths in erythropoietic forms.
Carry written documentation of porphyria and safe/unsafe drugs when travelling or accessing healthcare from unfamiliar providers.
Join a patient registry at a porphyria centre to enable long-term follow-up of complications including liver disease and risk of hepatocellular carcinoma.
Inform all healthcare providers — including dentists and anaesthetists — of the diagnosis before any procedure.

recommended: Regular meals with adequate carbohydrate (complex carbohydrates such as whole grains, potatoes, and legumes) to suppress ALA synthase 1 activity
Adequate protein and energy to prevent fasting-induced attacks
Calcium and vitamin D supplementation if GnRH agonists are used long-term for cyclical attacks
Iron-restricted diet in confirmed PCT with iron overload
avoid: Fasting, skipping meals, or very low calorie or ketogenic diets
Alcohol in all forms, especially in PCT
High-iron foods or iron supplements in PCT
Herbal supplements without first checking porphyria safety — many herbal preparations contain porphyrinogenic compounds

exerciseGuidelines: Moderate regular exercise is safe and beneficial for general health. Intense exercise during an acute attack is inappropriate given pain, weakness, and autonomic instability. Patients recovering from motor neuropathy require supervised physiotherapy for limb weakness. Between attacks, normal activity levels are appropriate. Adequate carbohydrate intake before and after exercise helps prevent exercise-induced caloric deficiency from triggering an attack.
dailyManagement: Follow the prescribed medication regimen and monthly givosiran injection schedule if receiving RNAi therapy.
Check every new medication against the porphyria drug database before taking it.
Keep a symptom diary — documenting prodromal pain, urine colour changes, and potential triggers — to identify personal precipitants.
Report any new or worsening abdominal pain, neurological symptoms, or dark urine to the specialist team promptly.
Attend regular follow-up appointments for liver function monitoring, as long-standing acute hepatic porphyria carries a small but real risk of hepatocellular carcinoma.
Undergo annual liver imaging (ultrasound) if advised by the specialist team.
prognosis: The prognosis of porphyria depends on the subtype and severity. Most gene carriers with acute hepatic porphyria never experience a symptomatic attack if they avoid known triggers; those who do attack usually recover fully with prompt hemin therapy if neuropathy has not developed. Motor neuropathy from delayed or inadequately treated attacks can be severe and partially reversible with rehabilitation, but axonal damage is not always fully repaired. With givosiran, approximately 74% of patients with recurrent attacks achieve sustained attack reduction, substantially improving quality of life. PCT has an excellent prognosis with phlebotomy or hydroxychloroquine; remission is maintained in the majority of patients who address underlying risk factors (iron overload, alcohol, hepatitis C). Long-standing acute hepatic porphyria carries an increased lifetime risk of hepatocellular carcinoma estimated at approximately 70-fold above the general population, though absolute rates remain low; regular liver surveillance is recommended.
recoveryTimeline: An uncomplicated acute attack treated promptly with hemin typically resolves over 3–7 days with resolution of abdominal pain and autonomic features. Neurological recovery after motor neuropathy can take weeks to months; severe or delayed cases may take one to two years for maximal recovery. PCT remission following phlebotomy typically takes 6–12 months. Givosiran's protective effect builds over the first three months of monthly dosing and is sustained with continued treatment.
complications: Motor neuropathy and respiratory failure — the most dangerous complication of an acute attack, requiring intensive care when respiratory muscles are affected.
Hyponatraemia from SIADH — potentially fatal electrolyte imbalance occurring during acute attacks and requiring careful, controlled correction.
Chronic kidney disease — kidneys accumulate porphyrin precursors over years of recurrent attacks; some patients with AIP develop progressive renal impairment.
Hepatocellular carcinoma — individuals with long-standing acute hepatic porphyria have an approximately 70-fold increased lifetime risk; regular ultrasound surveillance is recommended.
Permanent scarring and skin damage from cutaneous blistering in PCT, variegate porphyria, and erythropoietic porphyrias.
Liver cirrhosis in erythropoietic protoporphyria, where porphyrin accumulation in the liver can cause progressive hepatic fibrosis requiring transplantation.

supportResources.name: American Porphyria Foundation
supportResources.description: US-based patient advocacy and information resource offering drug safety databases, specialist centre directories, and patient forums.

supportResources.name: European Porphyria Network (EPNET)
supportResources.description: Network of European specialist porphyria centres providing drug safety databases, clinical guidelines, and a 24-hour emergency advice service.

supportResources.name: NIH National Institute of Diabetes and Digestive and Kidney Diseases — Porphyria
supportResources.description: Authoritative patient-facing overview of porphyria types, diagnosis, and treatment from the U.S. National Institutes of Health.

sources.title: Puy H, Gouya L, Deybach JC. Porphyrias. Lancet. 2010;375(9718):924-937.
Balwani M et al. Acute Hepatic Porphyrias: Recommendations for Evaluation and Long-Term Management. Hepatology. 2017;66(4):1314-1322.
Sardh E et al. Phase 1 Trial of an RNA Interference Therapy for Acute Intermittent Porphyria. NEJM. 2019;380(6):549-558.

American Porphyria Foundation — Acute Intermittent Porphyria

confusedWithConditions.name: Systemic lupus erythematosus
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: SLE causes photosensitive rash, multi-system disease, and positive ANA; porphyria produces blistering and scarring rather than the butterfly rash and is distinguished by elevated urine porphyrins, not autoantibodies.

confusedWithConditions.name: Anaemia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Anaemia is a common finding in erythropoietic porphyrias due to porphyrin-mediated red cell damage, but the photosensitivity, characteristic urine colour, and abnormal porphyrin fractions are not features of iron-deficiency or other nutritional anaemias.

confusedWithConditions.name: Acute intermittent porphyria
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: AIP (E80.21) is a specific confirmed subtype with identified HMBS mutation; E80.20 is used when the acute porphyria picture is present but the subtype has not yet been determined or confirmed genetically.

relatedConditions.name: Acute intermittent porphyria
relatedConditions.relevance: The most common named acute hepatic porphyria; the primary condition to consider when the diagnostic workup resolves from E80.20 to a specific subtype.

relatedConditions.name: Porphyria cutanea tarda
relatedConditions.relevance: The most common porphyria worldwide; often the leading specific diagnosis when cutaneous features predominate without acute attacks.

relatedConditions.name: Hereditary erythropoietic porphyria
relatedConditions.relevance: An erythropoietic subtype presenting from childhood with severe photosensitivity and haemolytic anaemia; in the differential when photosensitivity with haemolysis is found.

relatedConditions.name: Other porphyria
relatedConditions.relevance: E80.29 captures rare or mixed porphyria presentations; important differential alongside E80.20 when the biochemical pattern does not fit standard subtypes.

faqs.question: What is unspecified porphyria?
faqs.answer: Unspecified porphyria (ICD-10 E80.20) means the clinical and biochemical evidence points to a porphyria disorder but the specific enzyme deficiency and subtype have not yet been confirmed. Further testing with urine, plasma, and stool porphyrin fractions and genetic analysis typically identifies the subtype.

faqs.question: What are the main symptoms of porphyria?
faqs.answer: Acute porphyrias cause severe abdominal pain, tachycardia, neuropsychiatric disturbance, and dark urine during attacks. Cutaneous porphyrias cause blistering, scarring, and fragility of sun-exposed skin. Some forms — variegate porphyria and hereditary coproporphyria — produce both.

faqs.question: What triggers a porphyria attack?
faqs.answer: Common triggers include porphyrinogenic drugs (many antibiotics, anticonvulsants, barbiturates), hormonal fluctuations especially around menstruation, fasting or caloric restriction, alcohol, infections, and physiological stress. Identifying and avoiding personal triggers is a core part of management.

faqs.question: How is porphyria diagnosed?
faqs.answer: A spot urine porphobilinogen (PBG) test is the first-line test during an acute attack; a markedly elevated result confirms acute hepatic porphyria. Full subtype identification requires 24-hour urine porphyrins, plasma porphyrins, stool porphyrins, and genetic testing. Testing during symptoms is more reliable than between attacks.

faqs.question: What is the treatment for an acute porphyria attack?
faqs.answer: Mild attacks are treated with high-dose intravenous glucose. Moderate to severe attacks require intravenous hemin (heme arginate or hematin), which suppresses the enzyme driving precursor accumulation. Pain is managed with opioids; porphyrinogenic drugs must be stopped immediately. Hospital admission is required.

faqs.question: What drugs are unsafe in porphyria?
faqs.answer: Many drugs are unsafe, including barbiturates, sulfonamides, many anticonvulsants (phenytoin, carbamazepine), rifampicin, and numerous others. Every medication must be checked against a specialist database such as the European Porphyria Network drug list before prescribing or taking it.

faqs.question: Is porphyria hereditary?
faqs.answer: Most acute and some cutaneous porphyrias follow autosomal dominant inheritance; a parent with a confirmed mutation has a 50% chance of passing it to each child. Gene carriers may never have symptoms but need drug-safety counselling. First-degree relatives of confirmed cases should be offered genetic screening.

faqs.question: Can porphyria be cured?
faqs.answer: Porphyria cutanea tarda can achieve long-term remission with phlebotomy and treatment of underlying factors such as hepatitis C. Liver transplantation cures acute hepatic porphyria at the source of overproduction. Most other forms are managed rather than cured, with the goal of preventing attacks and monitoring complications.

faqs.question: What is givosiran and who is it for?
faqs.answer: Givosiran (Givlaari) is a monthly subcutaneous RNA interference injection that reduces hepatic ALA synthase 1 expression, preventing the enzyme upregulation that drives acute attacks. It is approved for adults with acute hepatic porphyria who have recurrent attacks despite avoidance of known triggers. Trial data show attack reduction of approximately 74%.

faqs.question: Why do porphyria attacks affect women more than men?
faqs.answer: Progesterone metabolites upregulate the enzyme ALA synthase 1, which drives accumulation of toxic precursors. This explains why acute attacks are roughly 5 times more common in women and often cluster in the premenstrual phase or with hormonal contraceptive use. Attacks rarely occur before puberty or after menopause.

faqs.question: Is there a cancer risk with porphyria?
faqs.answer: People with long-standing acute hepatic porphyria have an increased lifetime risk of hepatocellular carcinoma estimated at approximately 70-fold above the general population. The absolute risk is still relatively low, but specialist centres recommend regular liver ultrasound surveillance. Erythropoietic protoporphyria can also cause liver cirrhosis.

faqs.question: Can porphyria cause mental health symptoms?
faqs.answer: Yes. During acute attacks, neuropsychiatric symptoms including acute confusion, anxiety, agitation, and hallucinations are common and can mimic primary psychiatric disorders. These resolve as the attack is treated. Repeated severe attacks can contribute to ongoing anxiety and depression related to illness burden.

faqs.question: Is it safe to sunbathe with porphyria?
faqs.answer: Cutaneous porphyrias and the cutaneous components of mixed forms cause photosensitivity to visible light wavelengths, not just UV, making standard glass windows and most sunscreens insufficient protection. Physical photoprotection — clothing, wide-brim hats, and broad-spectrum high-SPF creams — is essential. Afamelanotide increases tolerance in erythropoietic protoporphyria.

faqs.question: How common is porphyria?
faqs.answer: Porphyria cutanea tarda is the most common form globally. Acute intermittent porphyria has a prevalence of roughly 1–2 per 100,000 in most populations, though gene carrier rates may be as high as 1 in 1,700 in some studies. All forms together affect approximately 1 in 25,000 in Western populations.

faqs.question: Can porphyria affect the kidneys?
faqs.answer: Yes. Chronic accumulation of porphyrin precursors over years of recurrent acute attacks is associated with progressive chronic kidney disease in some patients with acute hepatic porphyria. Renal function should be monitored at regular follow-up visits as part of long-term specialist care.

faqs.question: What should I do in a porphyria emergency?
faqs.answer: If you have an acute attack with worsening abdominal pain, new weakness, breathing difficulty, or confusion, go to an emergency department and tell staff you have porphyria. Contact your specialist porphyria centre immediately — most have 24-hour advice lines. Carry your porphyria drug-safety card so unsafe treatments can be avoided.

faqs.question: What does porphyria urine look like?
faqs.answer: During or after an acute attack, urine may appear dark red, port-wine, or brown due to high concentrations of porphyrin precursors. The colour often intensifies on standing or exposure to light. Normal-coloured urine between attacks does not rule out porphyria — urine PBG can be normal outside of episodes.

faqs.question: Does diet affect porphyria?
faqs.answer: Yes. Regular carbohydrate intake suppresses ALA synthase 1 and reduces attack frequency in acute forms. Fasting, skipping meals, or low-carbohydrate diets are among the most common precipitants of acute attacks. Iron-restricted diet is recommended in porphyria cutanea tarda. Alcohol should be avoided in all porphyria types.

faqs.question: What is the difference between hepatic and erythropoietic porphyria?
faqs.answer: Hepatic porphyrias arise from enzyme deficiency in the liver and typically cause acute neurovisceral attacks or cutaneous symptoms from uroporphyrins. Erythropoietic porphyrias arise in bone marrow red cell precursors and cause photosensitivity and haemolytic anaemia from infancy. The distinction matters because treatment strategies differ substantially.

metaDescription: Porphyria (E80.20) causes severe abdominal attacks and photosensitive skin from heme enzyme defects. Learn triggers, diagnosis, hemin treatment and prevention.
